claim: The yield strength can be increased by 30–40 MPa for every 0.1% of added C.
evidence_text: Some studies have shown that the yield strength can be increased by 30~40 MPa for every 0.1% of added C [12].

claim: κ-carbides are classified as intragranular and intergranular; intergranular κ-carbides adversely affect mechanical properties, while finely dispersed intragranular κ-carbides are beneficial and large intragranular κ-carbides are detrimental.
evidence_text: Based on the location of the precipitation, κ-carbides can be loosely divided into intragranular κ-carbides and intergranular κ-carbides. In general, intergranular κ-carbides adversely affect mechanical properties, while the influence of intragranular κ-carbides on mechanical properties is closely related to the size of the precipitates. Finely dispersed and well-distributed intragranular κ-carbides in an austenite matrix are beneficial for mechanical properties, whereas intragranular κ-carbides of large size are typically not conducive to improvements in overall mechanical properties.

claim: The ingots were homogenized at 1200 °C for 5 h and then hot rolled into rods with a diameter of 16 mm, followed by air cooling to room temperature.
evidence_text: The resulting ingots, covering a range of compositions, were homogenized at 1200 °C for 5 h, and then hot rolled into rods with a diameter of 16 mm, before being air-cooled to room temperature.

claim: The hot-rolled rods were solution-treated at 1050 °C or 1100 °C for 2 h followed by water quenching.
evidence_text: The resulting hot-rolled rods were solution-treated at temperatures of either 1050 °C–1100 °C for 2 h followed by water quenching.

claim: Tensile tests were performed on standard specimens with a gauge length of 25 mm and diameter of 5 mm at an initial strain rate of 1.1×10⁻³ s⁻¹ at ambient temperature.
evidence_text: Tension tests were carried out on standard tensile specimens with gauge length of 25 mm and diameter of 5 mm, cut from the water-quenched steel samples with the tensile axis parallel to the rod axis. The stress-strain responses and corresponding strain hardening rates of the solution-treated specimens were obtained from tensile tests carried out at an initial strain rate of 1.1×10−3 s−1 at ambient temperature using an MTS tensile machine.

claim: Charpy impact toughness was measured at -40 °C using an MTS impact tester with a 500 J capacity, with the V-notch perpendicular to the rod axis.
evidence_text: Samples for impact toughness measurements were cut from the solution-treated rods with the V-notch perpendicular to the rod axis. Charpy impact energies were measured using a MTS impact tester (500 J capacity) at -40 °C.

claim: As the carbon content increased from 0.6% to 1.5% in Fe30Mn11Al(x)C steel, the yield strength increased from 542 MPa to 1125 MPa, while tensile elongation decreased from ~60% to ~20%.
evidence_text: a strong increase in yield strength from 542 MPa to 1125 MPa is observed as the carbon content is increased from x = 0.6%–1.5% in a Fe30Mn11Al(x)C steel. This strength increase is, however, accompanied by a significant decrease in tensile elongation from ~60% to ~20%

claim: As the Al content increased from 6% to 12% in Fe30Mn(x)Al1C steel, the yield strength increased from 433 MPa to 846 MPa, while elongation decreased from ~75% to ~40%.
evidence_text: the yield strength increased from 433 MPa to 846 MPa as the Al content was raised from 6% to 12% in a Fe30Mn(x)Al1C steel (Fig. 2(c)), while the elongation decreased from ~75% to ~40%.

claim: Both the ultimate true strength (σTS) and fracture strength (σFS) did not vary significantly with alloying content, with mean values of around 1350 MPa and 2150 MPa, respectively.
evidence_text: both the σTS and σFS did not vary significantly with alloying content, with mean values of around 1350 MPa for the UTS and 2150 MPa for the σFS.

claim: The true stress at maximum load (σ_TS) and true fracture stress (σ_FS) do not vary significantly with alloying content, exhibiting mean values of approximately 1350 MPa and 2150 MPa, respectively.
evidence_text: both the $\sigma _ { T S }$ and $\sigma _ { F S }$ did not vary significantly with alloying content, with mean values of around 1350 MPa for the UTS and 2150 MPa for the $\sigma _ { F S }$ .

claim: No κ-carbides are observed in the steel with 8% Al; extensive κ-carbide formation occurs at Al ≥ 10%, with average diameters of about 1 nm at 10% Al, 1.5 nm at 11% Al, and 15 nm at 12% Al.
evidence_text: No κ-carbides were observed in the Fe30Mn(x)Al1C low density steel with 8% Al (Fig. 5(e)). In contrast extensive κ-carbide formation was observed for Al contents of 10% and larger (Fig. 5(f-h)). The average κ-carbide diameters were estimated as about 1 nm, 1.5 nm and 15 nm for the steels with 10%, 11% and 12% Al, respectively.

claim: The Fe30Mn12Al1C steel contains 3.1 vol% BCC ferrite and exhibits an austenite grain size of 12.6 µm, which is significantly refined compared to the fully austenitic steels with lower Al content.
evidence_text: while a small amount of ferrite phase, with a volume fraction of 3.1%, was observed in the low density steel alloyed with 12%Al. ... In contrast, the austenite grain size in the 12% Al steel (Fig. 5(d)) was significantly smaller (12.6 μm).

claim: After solution treatment at 1050°C, κ-carbides are uniformly distributed in the austenite; increasing C from 0.6% to 1.5% raises κ-carbide size from ~1 nm to ~50 nm, increasing Al from 8% to 12% raises size from 0.5 nm to 15 nm, while increasing Mn from 25% to 35% reduces size from 5 nm to 2 nm, demonstrating that C and Al strongly promote κ-carbide coarsening, whereas Mn has a weak refining effect.
evidence_text: The increase in C content from 0.6% to 1.5% (2.5-fold) results in a sharp increase in κ-carbide size from ~1 nm to ~50 nm (50-fold). Similarly, the rise in Al content from 8% to 12% (1.5-fold) leads to an increase in κ-carbide size from 0.5 nm to 15 nm (30-fold). By comparison, only a weak effect of Mn content on the κ-carbide size is observed in Fig. 6(a), where the increment in Mn content from 25% to 35% (1.4-fold) results in a decrease of κ-carbide size just from 5 nm to 2 nm (2.5-fold).

claim: The density of solution-treated Fe–Mn–Al–C low-density steels decreases linearly with alloying content and follows ρ (g/cm³) = 8.10 − 0.102C − 0.10Al − 0.01Mn; the C coefficient 0.102 g/cm³ per 1 wt% is much smaller than the previously reported 0.41 g/cm³ per 1 wt%, yielding accurate predictions for Fe30Mn11Al1.0C (6.6 g/cm³ vs. exp. 6.6 g/cm³) and Fe30Mn11Al1.5C (6.55 g/cm³ vs. exp. 6.56 g/cm³), whereas the previous coefficient underestimates density as 6.29 and 6.1 g/cm³, respectively.
evidence_text: A linear fit for each of the three elements suggests that the overall density can be described according to Eq. 3 ρ = 8.10 − 0.102C − 0.10Al − 0.01Mn (3). … For example, the density of the Fe30Mn11Al1⋅0C low-density steel is calculated from Eqn. (3) as 6.6 g/cm³, matching the value obtained in our experiments (Table 1), whereas the predicted density using the values given in Ref. [4] is just 6.29 g/cm³. Similarly, the density calculated using Eqn. (3) for Fe30Mn11Al1⋅5C (6.55 g/cm³) matches closely our experimentally determined value (6.56 g/cm³), whereas the density calculated using the values in Ref. [4] is just 6.1 g/cm³.

claim: The density of Fe30Mn11Al1.0C calculated with the new formula (Eqn. 3) is 6.6 g/cm³, matching the experimental value, whereas the previously proposed formula gives 6.29 g/cm³.
evidence_text: the density of the Fe30Mn11Al1⋅0C low-density steel is calculated from Eqn. (3) as 6.6 g/cm3 , matching the value obtained in our experiments (Table 1), whereas the predicted density using the values given in Ref. [4] is just 6.29 g/cm3.

claim: For Fe30Mn11Al1.5C, the density calculated with the new formula is 6.55 g/cm³, closely matching the experimental 6.56 g/cm³, while the previous formula gives 6.1 g/cm³.
evidence_text: Similarly, the density calculated using Eqn. (3) for Fe30Mn11Al1⋅5C (6.55 g/cm3 ) matches closely our experimentally determined value (6.56 g/cm3), whereas the density calculated using the values in Ref. [4] is just 6.1 g/cm3.

claim: Yield strength of solution-treated low-density steels is linearly proportional to the square root of κ-carbide size (d_Car)^0.5, with a fitted slope of 97.1 MPa/nm^0.5, indicating strong shear hardening.
evidence_text: Based on the linear fitting of yield strength dependence on the square root of κ-carbide size (Fig. 7(a)), a constant slope of 97.1 MPa/nm^1/2 is obtained, implying a strong shear hardening from the κ-carbides.

claim: The flow stress in the absence of κ-carbide strengthening is estimated as approximately 478 MPa, attributed to solid solution hardening and austenite grain size strengthening.
evidence_text: The flow stress in the absence of any strengthening contribution from κ-carbides was estimated as about 478 MPa (Fig. 7 (a)), which may be explained by both solid solution hardening and austenite grain size strengthening.

claim: Particle shear strengthening from κ-carbides remains active for sizes larger than 50 nm, substantially exceeding the typical ~5 nm critical size for transition to Orowan mechanism.
evidence_text: It should be pointed out that the data in Fig. 7(a) suggest that particle shear strengthening is still active for κ-carbide sizes larger as 50 nm, which is significantly larger than the typical ~5 nm critical precipitate size for the transition in strengthening from shearing mode to an Orowan mechanism.

claim: The strengthening contribution of coherent κ-carbides to austenite follows a particle shear model expressed as σ_shear = α f^1/2 d_Car^1/2 (Eq. (4)).
evidence_text: Thus, their strengthening contribution to the austenite phase can be formulated by a particle shear model [29,30], rather than by an Orowan mechanism [31]. The shear strength contribution from the κ-carbides can therefore be simply formulated by Eq. (4): σ_shear = α f^{1/2} d_Car^{1/2}.

claim: The tensile strength σ_TS is approximately 1350 MPa and fracture strength σ_FS is approximately 2150 MPa for all investigated steels, independent of C, Mn, Al content and microstructure.
evidence_text: The σ_TS was estimated as about 1350 MPa for all the designed low-density steels in the present study. Thus, microcracks will nucleate at this strength, and the σ_TS is not significantly affected by the C, Mn, and Al alloying content. Similarly, the σ_FS was estimated for all the investigated steels as about 2150 MPa (Table 2), implying that fracture strength also is not affected by the alloying content. Furthermore, this implies that both the σ_TS and σ_FS do not vary with microstructure,

claim: σ_FS and σ do not change significantly with variation in C, Mn and Al content after solution treatment at 1050°C, and similarly at 1100°C.
evidence_text: As discussed above the σ_FS and σ are do not change significantly with variation in C, Mn and Al content after solid solution treatment at 1050°C, and a similar result is also found for the samples solution treated at 1100°C (see Fig. 8(d)).

claim: The very similar values of σ_TS and σ_FS for low-density steels with and without κ-carbides cannot be explained by variations in κ-carbide size, and the origin remains unresolved.
evidence_text: One remaining unexplained issue, however, is the observation of very similar values of σ_TS and FRS for low density steels with and without κ-carbides, as this cannot be interpreted by variations in κ-carbide size.

claim: For all examined low-density steels, the ultimate true stress (σ_TS) and fracture true stress (σ_FS) are nearly constant at approximately 1350 MPa and 2150 MPa, respectively, irrespective of composition and the presence of κ-carbides.
evidence_text: the σ_TS and σ_FS for all the examined steels were estimated at only about 1350 MPa and 2150 MPa, respectively.

claim: The dependence of density on C, Mn, and Al content (mass%) in Fe-Mn-Al-C low-density steels is given by ρ = 8.1 – 0.102C – 0.10Al – 0.01Mn.
evidence_text: The dependence of density on the C, Mn, and Al content can be formulated as: ρ = 8.1 – 0.102C – 0.10Al – 0.01Mn, where C, Al and Mn represent the C, Al and Mn content in mass %.

claim: Fe30Mn10Al1C steel after 1050 °C solution treatment exhibits a yield strength of 642 MPa, total elongation of 72%, Charpy impact toughness at −40 °C of 247 J, and density of 6.69 g/cm³.
evidence_text: An excellent combination of yield strength of 642 MPa, ductility of 72%, and impact toughness at -40°C of 247 J in a steel with density of 6.69 g/cm^3, were obtained after 1050°C solution treatment of the Fe30Mn10Al1C steel.

claim: The yield strength contribution from κ-carbides is attributed to a shear strengthening mechanism, which significantly decreases the work hardening rate for κ-carbides with a diameter larger than 4 nm.
evidence_text: The yield strength contribution from κ-carbides is attributed to a shear strengthening mechanism, which allows an increase in yield strength, while significantly decreasing the work hardening rate for κ-carbides with diameter larger than 4 nm.

claim: Figure 8 presents the true tensile stress-strain and work hardening rate curves for Fe-Mn-Al-C steels with varying C (0.6–1.5%), Mn (20–35%), and Al (6–12%) contents after solution treatment at 1050 °C and 1100 °C.
evidence_text: Fig. 8. Work hardening behavior of low-density steels alloyed with different C, Mn, and Al content followed by solution heat treatment at the temperature, in which the solid lines and the dashed lines show the true tensile stress-strain (SS) curves and the work hardening (WH) rate curves, respectively: (a) effect of C content after solution treatment at 1050°C, (b) effect of Mn content after solution treatment at 1050°C, (c) effect of Al content after solution treatment at 1050°C, (d) effect of Al content after solution treatment at 1100°C.

claim: The nearly constant ultimate true stress (~1350 MPa) and fracture true stress (~2150 MPa) in steels with and without κ-carbides remain unexplained and may be related to unique microstructural evolution.
evidence_text: One remaining unexplained issue, however, is the observation of very similar values of σ_TS and FRS for low density steels with and without κ-carbides, as this cannot be interpreted by variations in κ-carbide size. The origin of the nearly unchanging values of σ_TS and σ_FS may be related to the unique microstructural evolution, and should be studied further in future work.
